data.title: Ambarella: Recent Pullback Still Not Enough To Make It A Buy
data.description:
On February 26, Ambarella, Inc. (AMBA) released its Q4 and FY 2025 results. Despite beating analyst consensus on revenue by $6 million and non-GAAP EPS by $0.13, the stock has taken a dive of 32% in six weeks before Trump's tariff announcement on Tuesday. The tariff threat certainly hasn't helped, but with industry bellwether Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) having dropped half as much during that time, it might be a good idea to review if AMBA has reached a level to consider it a buy. Particularly as the tariff threat from the United States is now known, this might be a buy the dip type of event for the semiconductor industry after the initial shock wave.

Ambarella is a developer of low-power system-on-a-chip semiconductors and software for edge artificial intelligence applications. The technologies enable electronic systems to become partially or fully autonomous, as it can detect and classify objects and people around them. The company has been using the technology to actively pursue the machine sensing market, ramping up that focus starting in 2023. Known as Edge AI, Ambarella integrates its legacy video processor technology with its CVflow architecture that supports a variety of AI inference algorithms in order to enable machines to detect the world around them. The most well-known application and prime target market for this technology would be autonomous vehicles.

AMBA tends to be more volatile than usual for a chip stock of its size. The automotive sector is perpetually five years away from vehicles that rival those on Futurama and The Jetsons. Despite the slower than hoped progression with EV and self-driving vehicles, AMBA achieved a solid 26% growth in revenue for the fiscal year ended January 2025. Revenue grew from $226.5 million to $284.9 million. Though it still needs to be pointed out that revenue was lower than its peak of over $330 million from two years ago:

Also of important note is that despite claims of positive EPS on an adjusted GAAP basis for Q4, AMBA hasn't come close to generating operating income in seven years. In my opinion, the losses have been generally going in the wrong direction as the company was closer to profitability in 2020 on a smaller revenue base than in 2025. AMBA has a generous stock compensation expense in excess of $100 million a year. This number accounts for the largest variance in GAAP versus non-GAAP EPS. If the company doesn't have the cash generating ability to buy back stock into treasury, all that stock compensation does is result in dilution. It's a real indication of the operating losses inherent in the business.

Midpoint revenue guidance for Q1 is $84 million. That would be an impressive 54% growth over last Q1's revenue of $54.5 million, though the company appears to have settled into a run rate of $80 to $85 million per quarter in revenue for the past two quarters. If it remains in this range, it would get back to revenue levels achieved in 2023. If it continues on its current growth trajectory, it could achieve north of $400 million in revenue. Though now that would seem to be a challenge given macroeconomic and political forces. Unfortunately, I don't expect even $400 million in revenue to be enough to achieve operating profits given the much higher levels of opex experienced in the last two years.

Even returning to revenue levels seen in 2023 may be a challenge given the current economic climate. The auto sector has been a particular target of tariff threats, already causing temporary production halts in North America as auto companies consider their next steps. Since those challenges are well known, AMBA and others in the industry have already taken a significant hit to their valuations.

AMBA gets a C- from Seeking Alpha's valuation grading system. It doesn't have any meaningful data on the P/E metrics given its negative GAAP earnings. On sales and cash flow multiples, it's often double or triple of the sector median. AMBA scores much better on year-over-year growth. But given that it's seeking to merely return to levels of revenue achieved in previous years, I don't see that as a great accomplishment.

One final thing to notice about AMBA over the last 10 years is that it historically reverts to a mean of about a 7-8x revenue multiple:

If someone was looking to hold AMBA based on historical valuation levels, now might be a good time. It hasn't traded significantly below 6x its revenue in 5 years, while it has spiked significantly above that level.

Conclusion: mixed bag of yellow and orange flags, so I hold a neutral or hold opinion

During my research on AMBA, I was debating back and forth whether to rate this stock a hold or a sell. The poor comparison to peers, endless operating losses and revenue growth that merely returns it to 2023 levels were turn-offs for me. However, the company does have operating momentum despite the drastic drop in stock price recently. It seems like a good bounce candidate for someone like me who thinks that after the initial shock of the tariff announcement, there will be a buy the dip movement. So I decided on a hold. Might as well wait for a bounce than sell at the bottom.

If you particularly believe in the automotive sector and the utility of Ambarella's Edge AI chip, then AMBA is trading at a level worthy of a buy and hold. If you are scouring the semis looking for a deal, there are better choices out there than this one.

data.title: 1 Ideal Buy From 23 "Safer" April Dividend Dogs In 50 Fortune World's Most Admired Companies (FWMAC)
data.description:
Foreword

Fortune collaborated with partner Korn Ferry on this survey of corporate reputations. It began with a universe of about 1,500 candidates: the 1,000 largest U.S. companies ranked by revenue, along with non-U.S. companies in Fortune’s Global 500 database that have revenues of $10 billion or more.

We winnowed the assortment to the highest-revenue companies in each industry, a total of 650 in 30 countries. The top-rated companies were picked from that pool of 650; the executives who voted work at the companies in that group.

To determine the best-regarded companies in 51 industries, Korn Ferry asked executives, directors, and analysts to rate enterprises in their own industry on nine criteria, from investment value and quality of management and products to social responsibility and ability to attract talent. A company’s score must rank in the top half of its industry survey to be listed.

To select our 50 All-Stars, Korn Ferry asked 3,380 executives, directors, and securities analysts who had responded to the industry surveys to select the 10 companies they admired most. They chose from a list made up of the companies that ranked in the top 25% in last year’s surveys, plus those that finished in the top 20% of their industry. Anyone could vote for any company in any industry.

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 50 Most Admired firms are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here, March 31 data focused on 43 dividend payers. The full list of 50 is posted in the Afterword text of this article.

Happily, 4 of the 41 dividend-paying most admired companies live up to the Dogcatcher “safer’ qualification showing free cash flow yields exceeding dividend yields. As of 3/31/25, they are: Bayerische Motoren Werke AG (OTC:BMWKY), Pfizer Inc (PFE), Nordstrom Inc (JWN), and Singapore Airlines (OTCPK:SINGY). Many first-time investors regard this condition as an invitation to look-closer.

One of the fourteen IDEAL was also deemed to have “safer” dividends whose free cash flow yield exceeds their dividend yield. That was: Nordstrom. Many first time buyers take this IDEAL signal as a “watch to Buy” sign. Unfortunately this IDEAL honor may be short-lived as Nordstrom agreed to go private back in December and will discontinue its dividend should that transition happen this year.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 23.65% To 105.03% Net Gains From Ten Top Fortune 50 WMA Companies To April, 2026

Four of ten top April Fortune 50 WMA company stocks by yield was among the top ten gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, the yield-based forecast for these Bloomberg top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 40% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, supplied the data points. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable gains as of March 31, 2025 were:

FWMACo Top Ten April Dividend Dogs By Net Gains

American Express (AXP) was projected to net $1,050.32 based on the median of target prices from 24 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 16% over the market as a whole.

Delta Air Lines (DAL) was projected to net $698.72 based on a median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 23% over the market as a whole.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) was projected to net $552.48 based on the median of target estimates from 10 analysts, plus its last dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 39% under the market as a whole.

Target Corp (TGT) was projected to net $333.73 based on dividends, plus the median of the target price estimates from 32 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% more than the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $297.50, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 34 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% greater than the market as a whole.

Toyota Motor (TM) was projected to net $294.60, based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 37% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $293.08 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 27 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% less than the market as a whole.

Bank of America (BAC) was projected to net $258.63, based on the median of target estimates from 21 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% more than the market as a whole.

United Parcel Service (UPS) was projected to net $241.57 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 33 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 11% over the market as a whole.

BlackRock (BLK) was projected to net $236.45, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% over the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 42.57% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 16% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

41 of 50 FWMACo Dogs Show April Analyst Target Gains

43 of 50 FWMACo Dogs Show Positive April Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top FWMACo Dogs By Yield For April

Top yield ten April 2025 FWMACo stocks represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Two consumer defensive representatives placed first and eighth, Bayaerisch Motoren Werke [1], and Nordstrom [8]. Thereafter, three health care stocks occupied the second, seventh and ninth ,places, Pfizer [2], Novo Nordisk [7], and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) [9].

Two industrials representatives placed third, tenth, United Parcel (UPS) [3], and Singapore Airlines [10].

Finally, two consumer staple stocks placed fourth and fifth, Target [4], and PepsiCo (PEP) [5]. to complete the top ten April list of Fortune 50 Worlds Most Admired stocks by yield.

Fortune 50 WMA Companies Costs per Dividend Dollar as of April, 25-6

The above chart reveals 43 Fortune Worlds Most Admired Companies subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the list is BMWKY at $12.35 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVDA at $2,710.03 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Ten of Forty-three April Dividend Paying FWMACo Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Ten dividend paying FWMACo stocks so infected are Bayeriesche Moteren Werke, PepsiCo, Morgan Stanley (MS), Singapore Airlines, Toyota, Coca-Cola (KO), Bank of America, JPMorgan Chase (JP), Goldman GS), and Eli Lilly (LLY). These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed uncertainty, and tariffs, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top-Yield FWMACo Dividend Dogs Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, four in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those four, already in the ideal zone, three more top-yield stock is within $40 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that those companies raise their dividends or do stock splits. Those actions may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to the sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 19.97% To 69.5% Top Ten FWMACo Upsides To April, 2026; (32) One -0.61% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 3.02% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 FWMACo Stocks As of April, 2026

Ten-top FWMACo Stocks were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten Bloomberg Dividend Focus stocks selected 3/3/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. Energy, health care, utilities, consumer discretionary, real estate, and technology missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend FWMACo Stocks (34) To Deliver 19.02% Vs. (35) 18.46% Net Gains by All Ten Come March 31, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Focus analyst 1-year targets to deliver 15.172% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, Vale, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 50.26%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield FWAMC dogs for April were: Singapore Airlines; Nordstrom; Pfizer; Bayerische Motoren Werke; Novo, with prices ranging from $10.02 to $69.44.

Five higher-priced FWMAC top-yield dogs as of March 31 were: Target; United Parcel; Morgan Stanley; PepsiCo; Johnson & Johnson, with prices ranging $104.36 to $165.84.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the market cools and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into 2025 with the exception of the few tech stocks, look for the last of the top ten FWMAC to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 1 Ideal Dog of 23 ‘Safer' FWMAC Stocks

Late March 2025, 23 of the 47 FWMAC stocks were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

YCharts 3/31/25 data showed one of twenty-three IDEAL dividend payers with dividends from $1,000 invested exceeded single share prices was also “Safer”: Nordstrom.

How 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Data Produced "Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1,000.00 invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend focus stocks conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. That one ideal “safer” dogs was Nordstrom.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

A market correction of 85% could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or authorize stock splits to bring price and dividends the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Footnotes

The Full List of Fortune 50 Worlds Most Admired Companies in 2025

Two stocks on the original Bloomberg 50 list were unavailable through YCharts sources. The listing above shows that one International bank and an Asian Airport Co were not listed on YCharts.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Bloomberg Dividend Focusdog updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net-gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Sustainable dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How Investors Should Approach Sweeping U.S. Tariffs, Global Fallout
data.description:
Markets around the world are digesting the new U.S. tariffs and the potential implications for global trade and growth. Michael Craig, Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives with TD Asset Management, looks at what these latest developments could mean for financial markets and how investors should be positioning themselves.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Markets have some clarity now on the Trump administration's tariff plan, and it has sparked a global sell-off. Joining us now for perspective is Michael Craig, Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives with TD Asset Management.

Michael, we've been waiting for this. We have the information. Clearly, there is a reaction in global markets. How are you reading all this?

Michael Craig: Well, there's two takes on it. There's the take you're hearing today. And it's, this is crazy and it's going to lead to global recession. Our sense is that this is kind of Trump to a tee in terms of asking for the world as a starting point of a negotiation.

Interesting yesterday that Mexico and Canada were not mentioned because our tariff negotiations are far more advanced, as we were the first countries to get hit. And so I think that this is the beginning of a negotiation. 10%, which is the baseline, is probably the floor. And the current levels are probably the ceiling.

There is risk that you get countermeasures by countries. There's a lot of rhetoric, and a lot of this is for domestic electorates. When you actually look at the bargaining position, it would be pretty dangerous to add, to reciprocate, just because the impact they're able to do on the US economy is far less than what the Americans can do to them.

So my sense is we'll start to see some negotiations. It means tariffs, ultimately, are going to come into play at some level but not at these levels. But, obviously, right now, the market has been caught offside and there's been a material sell-off.

Again, on the year, not massive. Stocks are down 7% on the year. Canada maybe a bit better than that. But it is certainly leading to a lot of uncertainty in the markets right now.

Greg Bonnell: If this is the opening round of negotiation to start to see country by country trying to negotiate, would we expect more volatility going forward? I just think about the amount of headlines that we're just inundated with all the time on this.

Michael Craig: Yes, for sure. I don't think this is going to be dealt with overnight. Our sense is we'd like to see progress, certainly by June and absolutely by September, such that it doesn't start to really filter into decisions that CFOs and companies are going to be making in terms of capital allocation. That could be a really negative long term if you start to see this drag on and companies really shy away, and then that starts to create a bit of a vicious circle.

So, the next six months are critical. And, ultimately, we would expect to see some negotiations happening right away.

Greg Bonnell: When we talk about capital allocation when it comes to investing, obviously we think about equities, fixed income, alternatives. I want to go through those buckets in terms of what we're going through right now, where we hope to end up. Let's start with equities.

Michael Craig: It's fairly negative across the board. What's interesting with equities, there are some longer-term implications. We're seeing it in our own country, as the discussion within our own election is really about investments and infrastructure and whatnot.

And various countries are going to have to look at rejigging their economic model away from exports to either consumption or investment. That is bullish. And so, the next little while is going to be very messy.

But longer term, we would be optimistic that equities will be just fine, as you're going to have actual positive fiscal impulse ex-US around the world in terms of reorientating their economies away from supplying goods to the US market. So this will take time, but that backdrop, I think, is bullish for the global stock markets.

The other thing I have to say is, the dollar is overvalued. Many currencies are undervalued. Today's reaction is interesting. The dollar is really under pressure, particularly against the euro and yen but also against the Canadian dollar.

And my sense is this is the beginning, perhaps, of a longer-term challenging or bear market in the US dollar as money gets repatriated in order to spend on things I talked about.

So, it's an interesting backdrop. I think we've had this era of exceptional US outperformance. I think we're going to start to see much less dispersion of returns or even a reversal where other markets are outperforming the US market. So that's something to think about for investors who are, for the most cases, overweight US assets but underweight kind of global stocks.

Greg Bonnell: What about fixed income? In this environment, is it going to do its job?

Michael Craig: In Canada today, it's actually not. Canadian bonds are modestly better today, but really only a few cents. Asian markets are quite strong. Europe is OK. And the US bond market is really rallying, although you're giving it all back because of the currency.

I think the market's sniffing out fiscal expansion. And so curves are steepening. Longer-term debt is not performing nearly as well as shorter-term debt. We are pricing in more potential cuts in the US market. We still have a couple priced in Canada. I think that is fair. But I am concerned about that longer debt trajectory.

And so, fixed income has been OK this year. It's positive. But there are some headwinds looking at the fixed income markets, particularly in Europe, if they do start to really follow through on fiscal expansion-- same for Japan.

Greg Bonnell: The traditional portfolio, we've covered the two big ones-- equities and fixed income-- hearing a lot of discussions, though, around alternatives. What do we think about alternatives in this environment?

Michael Craig: Yeah. So we've been using more alternatives over the last few years. They are exactly environments like this where they really do shine.

Not all alternatives are made equal. And so, when you're thinking about alternatives, you've really got to think about how it improves the overall diversification in the portfolio. So think things like infrastructure, real estate, commodities, mortgages are some of the things that we use. They're really creating an improved diversification profile for clients.

And, candidly right now, we're in a bit of a fog. I mean, I can give you my opinions, but tomorrow it might change materially based on what happens. It is what it is. The only real advantage we can have is by using better diversification to work through these environments and have overall drawdowns reduced.

Greg Bonnell: Is that the real key takeaway in turbulent times like these? In the end, as an investor, you need to think about diversification.

Michael Craig: You need to think about diversification. And investing, it's a long-term endeavor. All of us invest through our lives. And through a lifetime, you're going to have euphoric days when things are way too good, and you're going to have horrible days when they're horrible, like today.

It's really that destination of where you're going and ensuring that your portfolio is built to get to where you're trying to achieve, whether it's capital appreciation or living off your wealth or what have you. Everyone's situation is unique. So days like today, well, are upsetting.

And, look, people are facing not only a tough day in their portfolios, but many are worried about their employment. So this is not a great backdrop for people right now. My hope is that this is the peak of it, at least in the tariff side, and that we've got better days ahead, particularly in terms of how the country is shifting towards more of a growth mindset.

Original Post

data.title: Don't Miss The Ongoing Sector Rotation
data.description:
Introduction

Year-to-date, the S&P 500 is down. This shouldn't be news to anyone, as we have been in "correction territory" for a minute now. My current understanding of the chaos is that the market is in turmoil over tariff concerns, and generally more uncertainty of political and economic trajectories.

There are three events/trends that I am highlighting for context:

1. The Fed published its latest projections in March, and they are negative. The FOMC has revised its forecasts down for real GDP growth and up for PCE inflation. I've highlighted the revisions from the previous projections below.

2. The GDPNow forecast (or "nowcast" as they call it) is showing a 3.7% contraction in real GDP for Q1 2025. While this is just a projection, GDPNow has a fairly clean track record in the past of estimating the correct direction GDP will move, although it has a history of overestimating changes during crises.

While the GDPNow figure had been positive up until the end of February, it has only gone down since, as private markets are scrambling to scoop up imports before tariffs begin. This practice is called "front loading," and is common when buyers expect prices to rise in the future after a certain date, like they are expecting with President Trump's volatile trade policy.

Regardless of why real GDP growth forecasts are negative, it will likely cause a negative GDP reading for Q1. Fears of recession will then likely drive market volatility higher.

3. Speaking of market volatility, it has been elevating, with the VIX (an index that tracks the velocity of S&P 500 options, and thus, volatility) finding higher lows YTD.

None of this is good for stocks.

If you want to hear my thoughts on the economics side more, I suggest these two recent articles on tariffs and economic conditions.

  • Preparing for Trump's 'Liberation Day'
  • Recession Fears Are Overblown

Today, however, I would like to talk more directly about stocks and less directly about the economy.

What's Been Going On In The Market?

The first step to understanding what has happened in the markets these last few months is to look at sector performance.

While the S&P 500 (SPX) is down 4% YTD, most sectors are in the positive. The downtrend is primarily concentrated in the tech, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Meanwhile, energy is up nearly 11%, and utilities are up over 5% YTD.

However, the market isn't an even distribution across its sectors. It's very heavily weighted toward some, and very little toward others. I have criticized the market in the past for being so lopsided and top-heavy toward mega-cap tech firms, but no amount of my complaining will fix that the S&P 500 is over 1/3rd information technology stocks.

Even as the energy sector has rallied nearly as much as the tech sector has fallen, it is still less than 4% of the index. This means that its swings don't affect the market nearly as much as the tech sector does.

Speaking of mega-cap growth stocks that lead the market, they have done the worst out of all of these sectors, and have been pummelled by the market. The Magnificent Seven have done even worse.

Up And Down Days Diverge

Here is a chart of the change for this past quarter on "significant days," or days the S&P 500 moves more than 1% in either direction. The chart has >1% movements being marked in green, and < -1% days being marked in red.

Immediately, we see a divergence of sector performance. On down days, and in aggregate during these days with large market movements, the information technology and consumer discretionary sectors fall as well.

Throughout these days, we have seen a rise in utilities and consumer staples, which are traditionally considered defensive stocks.

When we chart the largest stocks in these categories, we can see that many of the stocks that are the weakest in this recent rout are those that had the strongest returns in 2024.

There are quite a few stocks in the weak list today that had decent or incredible run-ups over the past few years, including NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Google (GOOG), Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE), Oracle (ORCL), and Intel (INTC).

The 10-Year Yield Is Forecasting More Pain

Lately, I've also been doing a lot of Fed watching. Because of this, I've also been doing a lot of bond yield watching.

I know, my life is exhilarating.

When you track the S&P 500's performance against bond yields, you see something interesting. The market has been following the 10yr fairly consistently YTD, with the recent drop in yield corresponding to a recent drop in the market.

Despite yet another drop in yields, the market has bounced back in divergence. The trend isn't perfect, but we should expect the market to fall to meet the 10yr if the 10yr doesn't rise to meet the market. The only way it would rise is if expectations of inflation without a recession got significantly worse.

I expect the market to continue its volatility so long as we see volatility in the 10yr yield, which has been all over the place.

It's mostly in flux because of uncertainty around the Fed, trade policy's impacts on inflation, and the trajectory of the economy, all things we discussed earlier.

Since those issues are all ongoing, and unlikely to be solved anytime soon (at least through the next quarter, if not the rest of the year+), investors should consider that the current path the market is on, it may stay on. We are likely entering a new market, one that is undergoing a re-pricing.

Tech itself may be being re-priced as we see forecasts for future earnings drop due to a decline in ex-U.S. sales.

Where I'm Investing

I've made three major moves in my own portfolio due to the changing market conditions. The first is to be overweight in non-tech sectors, and to diversify my holdings across traditionally defensive firms that have been fairing significantly better than the market.

Equity Income Funds

To that end, I have been recommending the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) for folks seeking equity exposure. This is a fund that holds a basket of defensive, dividend-paying value stocks (tech is still in there, but it's significantly smaller) and sells options against them for income. This fund will benefit from both the ongoing sector rotation, but also from the increased volatility of the markets.

You can see my explicit thoughts on JEPI from this recent article.

As mentioned in my recent commentary on tariff plans, I am not a buyer of equities right now; if I were, I'd be buying JEPI, however. I believe that we are not done with the current market tantrum, and that the rally we've seen so far in the past few days is fleeting. I'm holding off on adding any, and just sitting on what equities I do have.

Cash Is King

I am not in "sell everything" mode, but I am deploying all new capital into two assets: cash and alternatives.

Currently, that is coming in the form of the 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV), which is a fund that effectively holds T-Bills and rotates them for you. This means it can be used as a liquid cash park. I covered it in detail recently.

Loading Up On Alternatives

The primary asset I have been adding to is managed futures, which, I believe, are set up to benefit from rising commodities prices (charged up by protectionist trade policies), as well as increased volatility in general. My managed futures fund of choice is the Simplify Managed Futures ETF (CTA).

I have been loading up on this fund, and believe that it will outperform over the next quarter, if not longer, dependent on how economic data influences the Fed in the future. CTA is an adaptable, actively managed fund sub-advised by commodities trading adviser, Altis Partners. It's gone back and forth with the S&P 500 over its lifespan, typically outperforming during down and volatile market regimes, and underperforming during stock bull runs.

Conclusion

Based on current data, and given how the market has reacted to politics over recent weeks, we can confidently say that there is some level of sector rotation going on, with money leaving risky sectors like tech and discretionary products and moving to defensive sectors like utilities and staples. Energy is also up, driven primarily by money looking for places to hide while the market throws a tantrum.

That tantrum is unlikely to end soon, with the catalysts for it still ongoing and not slowing down, like the U.S. trade war, re-pricing of global profits, and an economy that is now projected to begin contracting this quarter.

None of this is good for stocks, but it is particularly worse for some stocks. To that end, I am recommending investors look to cash and alternatives like managed futures to deploy current capital while we wait out the instability.

For investors determined to stay in the markets, I am recommending covered call funds to take advantage of potentially flat or down markets, where the increased volatility could lead to outperformance over long-only indices. Particularly, I am recommending funds that are heavier on defensive stocks, since I see more upside for them as the rotation continues.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Steel Dynamics: Relatively Well Positioned For Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Shares of Steel Dynamics, Inc. (NASDAQ:STLD) plunged 9% on Thursday as investors reacted to President Trump’s reciprocal tariff announcement, and shares are now moving back toward a 52-week low. As a domestic steel producer, STLD does not have meaningful direct tariff exposure, insofar as imported steel becomes more expensive, that would help its competitive positioning. However, fears that tariffs will reduce US manufacturing activity and thereby demand from STLD’s customers caused the negative share price reaction.

I last covered shares of Steel Dynamics in December, rating them a “buy,” and even after their Thursday drop, the stock is flat since that recommendation, meaningfully outperforming the 10% drop in the S&P 500. Given the significant shifts in the macro environment, now is a good time to revisit STLD and see if investors should sell out of STLD, given the lack of damage to shares year to date. I still view STLD as a long-term winner.

Historically, about half of Steel Dynamics’s business was construction-related and half manufacturing/industrial, though given recent organic investments, its exposure to the auto sector has grown. All else equal, tariffs will help restrict the supply of foreign products to the US, which will improve Steel Dynamics’s relative positioning and ability to supply US projects. However, with manufacturers and builders facing potentially higher input costs, they may reduce activity.

In the long-term, if tariffs lead to reshoring, that would be a positive for STLD as those factories will need steel to be constructed. If tariffs remain in place, that may occur. In the near term, there is tremendous uncertainty, as these tariffs may or may not prove permanent. Until there is more clarity, reshoring may not occur, and there could be a chilling effect on business investment, creating a headwind for STLD.

Manufacturers may also respond to higher input costs by cutting production, so while STLD may be able to retain or slightly expand its margin, it will face lower volumes, which in turn could impact profitability. Additionally, some of its product is exported. For instance, its Sinton plant sends 30% to Mexico, some of which is for autos and products, which in turn are imported back into the US. It is still unclear what the US relationship with Mexico and Canada will be, which could hinder this activity.

For Steel Dynamics, the impact of tariffs is complex and unclear, with both positives and negatives. In the near term, I do view US steelmakers as increasingly protected from international competition, but I see downside risk to volumes amid pricing pressure, weak consumer sentiment, and tremendous policy uncertainty. In the long-term, reshoring will be positive for the business, but this is more likely to benefit results in 3-5 years than over the next year.

Even before tariffs, the company was facing some pressure. In March, Steel Dynamics pre-announced that Q1 results would be below consensus at $1.36-$1.40, roughly flat sequentially. Looking deeper into its disclosures, results from its steel operations should increase sequentially, given increased shipments, partially offset by lower margins. It sells some steel under long-term contracts, and pricing has lagged the increase in spot prices, which should catch up in coming quarters. It has seen growth from energy, construction, and auto customers.

Offsetting this, profits from steel fabrications are set to fall, though some of this decline is due to the seasonal decline in shipments. Pricing was also down 5%, given weak end markets. Orders have improved, and its backlog stretches into Q3, aided by demand from data center construction. However, if reports about Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) are true, data center demand may not prove as robust as once hoped.

With results fairly well known, the focus will be on the outlook when the company reports results on April 22nd. I would expect management to speak favorably on tariffs long term, while advocating for more protection for the US steel industry. I do expect it to warn about weaker demand, particularly from auto and manufacturing customers.

I will also be listening closely to clearer guidance on its major growth projects, which should begin contributing to results this year. Its $1.9 billion Sinton Texas steel mill is now operating and has reached 90% of anticipated production levels. It expects the mill to contribute to profits in Q2. Steel Dynamics also has a $2.7 billion new aluminum plant. In the Q1 update, management said it expects to begin shipping material “mid-2025.” This appears slightly later than its prior “plan to produce commercially viable products before mid-year 2025.”

Ultimately, I do not view one or two months of timing changes as material in the long run, though it could swing this year’s EBITDA by ~$25 million. Once up and running, STLD expects mid-cycle EBITDA of $650-700 million. Its product mix will be 45% cans and 35% autos; can demand should be fairly resilient while I see risk in autos. I view the product diversification into aluminum favorably, given the limited domestic supply.

Bigger picture, even if these plants provide below mid-cycle EBITDA for the company in 2025/2026, given a downbeat economy, we should be at a cash flow inflection point. STLD has spent $4.6 billion building these, and its cap-ex spending should fall dramatically from the $1.8 billion spent last year over the next two years. At the same time, they will begin contributing some EBITDA. Just by ending its investment cycle, we should see $1 billion or more of free cash flow improvement from its breakeven performance in 2024. This alone greatly insulates the company from a potential downturn in demand.

It is also worth emphasizing again that even before these tariffs-related pressures, the company had passed peak earnings. In Q4, revenue fell by about $360 million, but the cost of goods sold declined by only $70 million, causing by itself about a $1.50 per share drop in earnings from last year. The average selling price per ton of steel fell by 7% to $1,011. As you can see, steel shipments have stayed near record levels, but profits are well below levels seen in 2022 (which were unsustainably strong amid supply chain shortages and government stimulus).

In its fabrication unit, given pressure from autos, we have seen volumes come down, and there has been a similar downshift in profitability. I would note that steel fabrication contributes a similar profit as steel operations, $142 million vs $165 million in Q4, on a fraction of the sales, $396 million vs $2.6 billion. That is because STLD is manufacturing specific parts for manufacturers, selling a value-add product vs just raw steel, greatly increasing margins.

Importantly, STLD maintains a very conservative balance sheet, with leverage below 1x, well below long-term targets. This leaves the company very well positioned to weather a worse downturn than I fear while returning capital to shareholders. Indeed, in 2024 as it earned $9.84, it bought back $1.2 billion of stock, reducing its share count by 6%.

Capital returns are likely to continue. In February, STLD increased its dividend by 9%, giving shares a 1.6% yield. As part of this announcement, it also added $1.5 billion to its share buyback authorization. Through March 12th, STLD bought back $191 million of stock. Buybacks should reduce its share count by at least 4% this year.

That means that if steel results were steady, STLD would earn $10.25 in 2025. The ramping of its Texas and aluminum facilities should also provide a $2 benefit relative to last year, meaning run-rate earnings are closer to $12.25. When I last covered STLD, I saw $9 of 2025 earnings. That would roughly imply a $600 million drop in existing steel gross margins, about 20%. From 2023 to 2024, gross margins fell $1.2 billion, so this would be roughly half the drop seen last year.

While there is significant uncertainty, I still view this as a reasonable base case. Even in a modest downturn, this company should be able to generate at least $6 per share in free cash flow in 2026, just given the normalization in cap-ex needs. Ultimately, it appears STLD has completed its expansion efforts just in time.

While the near-term outlook has become incredibly uncertain, I still view the steelmakers as best positioned in the supply chain, given protection from imported competition. That should help preserve profits even if we do see business investment slow. I had seen shares moving toward $135 to reflect the contribution of its new plants, but in this environment, that price target is likely too aggressive to be achieved in 6-12 months.

Still, I view 12x earnings for a cash-generative business, enjoying idiosyncratic cash flow improvement aside from the macro, as attractive. Within the manufacturing sector, I view STLD as among the best opportunities in this period of uncertainty, and I would be a buyer, even as it may take longer than 12 months to reach fair value.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Buy Aerospace And Defense On Tariff Drama
data.description:
The markets are currently in the red, and that's understandable given the trade turmoil. However, I do believe that this opens up opportunities for companies in the defense industry. Lockheed Martin stock has lost 26% measured from its high in October 2024. Recently, Lockheed Martin lost the contest for the NGAD (Next-Generation Air Dominance) program from Boeing (BA) and that did not help the stock price either.

However, I do believe that the stock price may be punished a bit too much, which makes it interesting to assess whether there is an attractive entry point. I do believe there are various reasons why Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) stock might be more attractive to investors now.

Lockheed Martin Was Never Expected To Win NGAD Contract

While investors were hoping that Lockheed Martin would win the contract for NGAD, I believe the reality is that the company was never likely to win the contract. Since the company is already a primary contractor for the $2 trillion F-35 program, it would give Lockheed Martin a virtual monopoly on fighter jet manufacturing. The US Air Force and US Navy have a history of not putting their eggs in one basket, and that simply means that Lockheed Martin did not have the bigger chances to win the contract.

While this would be a significant growth opportunity for Lockheed Martin, I do believe that investors should have informed themselves better on the likelihood of Lockheed Martin winning the contract.

Tariffs May Shift Investor Focus Back To Defense

Over the past few months, investor sentiment for US defense stocks has taken a hit. That was exacerbated by the White House clash between President Volodymyr Zelensky and President Donald Trump and Vice President JD Vance. It led to European countries focusing more on European companies for their defense needs.

However, I do believe that the current trade environment is going to be extremely harmful to the price of consumer goods, and that may shift some focus back to defense. For instance, the F-35 is a multinational program but the way the program is structured it should be exempt from tariffs when parts cross the border. Furthermore, for many defense sales, the US government acts as an intermediary through the FMS (Foreign Military Sales) program. While defense equipment is not completely shielded from tariffs, it's definitely more shielded than consumer goods and products for the consumer market.

I believe that's also one of the reasons why pure defense plays did not sell off as sharply during the market sell off following the announcement of reciprocal tariffs.

Defense Budget Direction: Higher, Not Lower

That the White House Administration is rather inconsistent with their policies and objectives is clear when looking at the defense budget environment. Cuts to the budget of 8% are what the administration is looking for. What should be kept in mind is that just from simply reducing the complexity of procurement and the way the procuring partners set up requirements, there could already be a significant reduction in costs. There likely will be some degree of prioritizing certain programs over others, but there are also savings possible by simply cutting complexity, meaning that less dollars are spent on ponderous administrative procedures.

The next-generation fighter jet award also shows continued commitment to expanding defense capabilities. Additionally, the US Secretary of State has been looking to NATO countries including the US to increase defense budgets to 5% of GDP. So, we initially had a push for lower budgets that seemed to indicate a defense budget reduction of 50%. That later reduced to 8% cuts, and now the administration is aligned on an increase to 5% of GDP. Data from the World Bank puts the most recent defense spendings as a percentage of GDP at 3.4%.

Obviously, with the current trade environment, GDP may decline, but as a percentage, the target is to increase defense budgets and I do believe in absolute terms that is the objective. Higher defense budgets, which I do believe remain required given the threat environment, are an obvious positive for defense contractors.

What Are Analysts Expecting For Lockheed Martin?

Lockheed Martin will report earnings on April 22. Analysts are expecting $17.8 billion in revenues and $6.30 in core earnings per share. This would indicate 3.6% year-on-year growth in revenues and flat EPS.

Lockheed Martin Stock Remains Compelling

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I do believe that Lockheed Martin stock could become more appealing in the current trading and investing environment. While one may have the impression that earnings of defense contractors are going to tank due to budget cuts, analysts are expecting something completely different.

Between 2024 and 2027, EBITDA is expected to grow by 9.8% annually and free cash flow is expected to grow in line with that. In February, I assigned a $530 price target to the stock and with the most recent estimates in which are largely unchanged and the most recent median trading multiples implemented, I am increasing the price target to $535.85 implying 18% upside.

So, there may very well be an attractive opportunity on diverging sentiment and fundamentals.

Conclusion: Tariffs Could Shift Focus To Defense

Defense is often considered as a stable growth opportunity, somewhat unlinked to short-term downturns in macroeconomics. With the tariffs in mind, I do believe that investors may direct their attention to the safe haven that defense has often been for investors. Defense equipment, in many cases, has a less global footprint, and even if it does, they're often to a major extent exempt from tariffs. And even if there are tariffs - governments are the ones that collect as well as pay the tariffs, making it a “left pocket, right pocket” story.

So, that makes defense relatively attractive for investment, and even more so when considering that the indications now are that defense budgets have to grow and not decline. Furthermore, Lockheed Martin is a pure defense play with less commercial exposure, which could help the stock price. As a result, I'm maintaining my buy rating and lifting my price target for Lockheed Martin stock.

data.title: Trump Playing Chicken With The World?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump's draconian tariffs, markets fall (0:30). Declines in retail and tech (2:50). Tesla stock price, Q1 deliveries, Musk murmurs (6:20). Retail bright spots and low lights (9:30). AI spending, guidance coming (13:20). Macro data points (15:25).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome back to Wall Street Breakfast. We're in some times, aren't we friend?

Brian Stewart: Indeed, yeah. Busy times. Busy times.

RS: Busy times, especially as the director of news. So tariff talk all around. What do you got for us, what would you share with investors?

BS: So we're recording this on Thursday as the markets fall following the tariff announcement last night. The tariffs were much more draconian than people had predicted. So the market is adjusting accordingly.

If you're taking the optimistic view of the process that we're now involved in, Trump is in the the process of doing a negotiation tactic. He's playing chicken with the rest of the world. These are the opening gambit to negotiations. And so over the next few weeks, we're gonna see these mitigated or exceptions made to the point where they won't be as aggressive as they are now.

The pessimistic view is that Trump isn't interested in coming to any sort of reasonable terms with countries, that there's a more kind of political component to the tariffs more than economic, and that they could conceivably spark a worldwide recession in the near future.

RS: What are the things that investors should be most paying attention to as they navigate these unprecedented times, in this market volatility?

BS: I think that the news flow over the next foreseeable future is going to be kind of how sticky are these tariffs? Is this the end state that we're just going to put these tariffs in place.

Just to give some perspective to listeners who haven't dug into the details yet, it's 10% a minimum, every country gets at least a 10% tariff. And then through an equation related to the trade deficit that we have with various countries, a tariff rate set.

And so the highest rates on countries like Cambodia is 49%. I believe doing this off the top of my head, but I think China was 34% on top of the 20% that we'd already put in place. So extremely high in normal economic structures.

The tariffs are just gonna be passed on to consumers. So you can expect items that are coming in from those countries to just become 49% more expensive or 34% more expensive depending on where they come from.

So as we look at the fallout today, unsurprisingly, you see a lot of declines in the retail sector, especially in the smaller cap and mid cap retail space. So just to give some examples, like Five Below (FIVE), a low price retailer that gets a lot of items from overseas, China, Taiwan, Vietnam, those kinds of places. It's down 26% as we're speaking.

Gap (GAP) is down 18%. Kohl's (KSS) is down 24%. Victoria's Secret (VSCO) down 20%. So companies that have a supply chain that's very, very reliant on those countries are going to to see a hard hit.

We're also seeing a lot of tech stocks get hit. One of the the standouts is apple, which is down about 8%, getting back to levels last seen last summer. Also just kind of doing a quick check of the magnificent seven.

We have Amazon (AMZN) down 7% and video down six, Meta (META) down six. They're all reaching September levels.

One, I guess you'd call it a standout in terms of how well it's holding up today is Microsoft (MSFT). It's only down 1% today, but it's just off a fifty two week low down 10% so far this year. So it's already priced in some of this action.

So we're seeing a broad based focused on consumer and technology stocks. As, like I said, we're just kind of adjusting to the reality and kind of trying to figure out what the reality is gonna be going forward.

RS: Before we leave the tariff conversation in favor of some stock specific news, anything noteworthy to point out about the tariffs on the uninhabited islands?

BS: Any process by which these tariffs were arrived at, it seems to be that there was a spreadsheet somewhere, and there was equations used and sort of spit out. And there wasn't a review process to see the reality of the situation on the ground and what this would mean and what this meant.

If you wanna look at it as a glimpse into the Trump administration's process to putting these in place, and if you're an optimist, maybe that points to the idea that this is just an opening gambit that we were setting a number, we needed that number to be justified in some sort of way, so here it is.

And now we're going to talk to the individual countries. Obviously you can't talk to an uninhabited island, but those tariffs aren't going to affect consumers anyway.

So, we'll see how things shake out. Generally speaking, looking back at past administrations, they've been very responsive to the stock market.

That's one of the kind of main signals that presidential administrations have used to determine popularity in the absence of when elections are far away.

If you want to judge popular opinion, the stock market is one of the indexes that they use. So traditionally, historically, a move like today would have the White House discussing internally next moves.

I'm not sure how responsive the Trump administration is. We just don't know. The administration's new. It hasn't changed policy much in response to previous stock market dips so far this year.

So it remains to be seen exactly what the next steps are.

RS: So speaking of not knowing what the next steps are and having a lot of drama around the numbers and volatility are the Tesla (TSLA) Q1 delivery numbers that we saw yesterday.

We did a special Bull Bear Investing Experts podcast episode around the stock. There was some questioning of why the stock was moving up. Was it because of that Politico report?

Is Elon Musk leaving DOGE? He says no. Hard to know exactly which sources to read when. But what would you say out of those numbers, and what would you say for Tesla followers?

BS: So just to review the action yesterday. Tesla was down early in the day based on the delivery numbers. As you point out, there was a bounce back later and the stock was higher, ended higher, before being down today, after the tariff announcement.

I think part of that was there was already an expectation in the market that the Q1 deliveries were gonna be bad. They were worse than expected. And so you saw a dip, but a lot of that already been priced into the stock.

And so I don't know that deliveries were ever going to have a lingering impact on the stock price. I think that was always going to be the jumping off point for whatever was coming next.

I do think that Politico story did provide a catalyst on the upside. I'm using Dan Ives now as a bull sentiment index. He's been pounding the table that Musk's activity in DOGE have been very problematic for Tesla's brands and that he needs to step away from DOGE and come back to the company and take the steps necessary.

He calls it a moment of truth for Musk. So if you take that as the bulls' view of the stock is that's a necessary step. You can see why the stock would rally on the idea that he was stepping back from DOGE and moving back to full time managements of Tesla later.

After that report came out, both the White House and Musk denied that it was true. It's not clear how much of that is saving face, how much of that is truly Musk's intent? The bottom line is it's not clear right now what Musk's plans are going forward, how much time he plans to spend at DOGE, how much time he plans to be at Tesla.

That uncertainty itself is a problem for Tesla. So a lot of the future of Tesla depends, and I'm talking about the near term future, if you're a long term bull, you're betting on autonomous driving, you're betting on robots, you're betting on the building out of the green infrastructure, solar and charging stations and all the parts of Tesla that point to a bright future.

But in the near term and the medium term, Musk is so tied to the company that's it is dependent, the movement of the stock in the near term is gonna be very dependent on how he handles the public outcry.

RS: Certainly interesting to see what happens there. What would you say as it pertains to retail? We touched on it at the beginning of the conversation. We saw a lot of movement out of some retail names, specifically the lower cost players like TJ Maxx (TJX), Costco (COST). What would you say or what else would you say about the retail side of things?

BS: You point out some bright spots today in the retail sector. I guess, TJX would be the ticker, that grouping of companies is TJ Maxx, Marshalls, that company, it's up 1.6% today. It was up 6% going into today in the previous week.

So the stock was already boosted. One of the benefits it has, it basically buys inventory from other retailers. So when companies have too much inventory, it'll take it off its hand and sell it at discounted prices.

That's its business plan. Because it has this flexible supply chain, it's not as plugged into, it can move around in response to these tariffs.

It's also benefiting from the general move to lower price options. So it's been one of the standout winners in the retail space today and in the near term.

You also see companies like Costco and Walmart (WMT). Costco's up slightly today and Walmart's down slightly, but down much less than the market in general.

Also, Walmart's up about 5% over the previous five days anyway. Walmart took a dip back in February after it issued soft guidance. But in recent days, it's reportedly been pushing its Chinese suppliers to lower costs.

So I think there's a bet that companies like Costco and Walmart are in a situation where all retailers are going to be hurt by these tariffs because their supply chains are going to be affected by much more expensive.

Companies like Costco and Walmart are large enough that they can negotiate better prices. So even though the entire industry is gonna be a general loser relative to their competitors, Walmart and Costco can get better deals, and so therefore, it can still offer lower prices.

RS: And what would you say about the stocks that have been down this week? Restoration Hardware (RH) is a big one, but many of the tech names, as you mentioned, are also significantly down.

How would you contextualize that for investors?

BS: So RH restoration hardware is down about 39% today, but, I mean, that's partially tariff related, but it had negative earnings as well.

It missed expectations and gave soft guidance. It's described the current housing market as the worst housing market in almost fifty years.

So it's a luxury furniture retailer, and it's just in the perfect storm. Everything's going wrong macro speaking for RH. And so you can see it losing a large chunk of its value today.

In terms of the other tech companies, besides the supply chain issues that are being affected by the tariffs, there's also the fear that the AI build out is not gonna be as aggressive as originally hoped.

I think there's just a general moving away. There was already a general moving away from tech into more defensive. People were hedging their bets a little bit on tech.

And so I think this has only exacerbated this, as we discussed during the sell off that happened earlier this year. If you're bullish, long term bullish generally, the best case scenario is that it falls quickly and finds a bottom quickly and then starts to recover.

So in a certain sense, if you want to take a reversely positive look at a day when the NASDAQ at its lowest point was down more than 5%, you can say we washed out all the concerns and now we can have a better base to build on.

So if you're a tech bull, this is a great chance to get tech companies at a reduced price. If you're convinced that we're headed straight into an economic brick wall, then this is just the beginning.

RS: And is there a timeline for when we might know more about AI spending? What do you sense out of that, or what are you seeing from that?

BS: Next week, we start to get the earning season churning. Next week is financial focus. There's not a lot of tech companies on the docket, but in the weeks following.

So as we get into the tail of April, we should start to get more tech companies reporting. The commentary from those tech companies is probably gonna be pretty cloudy.

I think a lot of them are going to have to suspend guidance or generalize their guidance in a way that makes it difficult to get visibility just because they're all gonna be scrambling to figure out the tariff situation.

But within those earnings reports and the commentary that the management's gonna provide, there should be kind of line items about underlying AI demand.

And so I think investors should look at that for longer term investment planning, looking for those nuggets of information that are gonna come out of these earnings reports about the underlying demand for infrastructure build out.

RS: And what would you say about the financial earnings coming?

BS: I feel that the market's gonna be very macro focused for a while. So I think individual companies, there might be large positive surprises or large negative surprises.

However, in general, I don't know if that's going to provide a catalyst to move the overall market. Cause I think it will be just so obsessed with the political machinations that are going to surround the tariff announcements.

But I do see it as a way, a signpost as we're going to start getting more earnings related data, financial related data in general. So I think the market's going to, over the next few weeks, start the process of turning inward a little bit and taking its eyes off of Washington and more on Wall Street in general.

RS: Oh, it'd be nice to take our eyes off Washington a little bit, won't it?

Last week we talked about the importance of some employment data that we'd be getting this past week. Also, the manufacturing surveys coming out. What would you say about what we saw and what macro points are coming in the coming week?

BS: So next week, we got the FOME minutes, which are gonna let us know what the Fed was talking about and thinking about when it made its last rate announcements.

We're also gonna get the CPI and PPIs. We're gonna get the latest inflation data.

I think the inflation data, even more than the Jobs report or the manufacturing report, I feel that given the situation with tariffs and the possible economic implications of those, that that data is gonna be seen as backward looking.

It just does not describe the reality that we're living in post tariffs. So I don't know that that's gonna have a huge impact on the thought process, but the CPI report might because it will kind of define the baseline as we look to whether or not tariffs are gonna cause higher prices.

I mean, the worst case scenario still remains that we end up in some sort of stagflationary environment where demand craters because everything gets too expensive.

And so we have a recession, but prices remain sticky because tariffs are adding to already existing inflationary pressures. And so prices remain high and jobs start to bottom out. So we could see a worst case scenario brewing. So I think that the inflation report that comes out next week will be very important in setting those expectations.

RS: And the employment picture?

BS: I just don't think that this month's data means much because so much has happened on a macro level.

There's been such a seismic shift in the possibilities, but I think if we look ahead, if we go to May when we're gonna get the April report, that's businesses that are going to be responding in the next few weeks to the realities of what their supply chain is going to look like, what their cost basis is going to be and setting their employment levels there.

So that would be the first data point that would involve companies responding to the new reality.

RS: And any other upcoming earnings to make note of?

BS: Another one next week is Delta Airlines (DAL). The first airline one, it'll be interesting just for the airlines. It's the first marker of demand for travel.

Also, CarMax (KMX) is an interesting one just because we are getting a 25% tariff on foreign made auto and auto parts. And so CarMax, which is a car dealer, it'd be interesting to see if there's a run on the bank for cars made before the tariffs put into place.

So just in terms of a macro data point, it might be interesting to check that out.

data.title: Tariffs Aren't As Scary For Tesla: Upgrading To Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

For anyone wondering, I said I liked Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) at $400, and again at $260, and then again at $222. I’m upgrading the stock to a strong buy as the sell-off continues, and my thesis stands. Funny enough, though, if you bought the stock on my last buy recommendation, you’d be up around 6% now, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), down over 5.4% during the same period.

And trust me, it’s not easy advocating for a stock with the heavily critiqued CEO Elon Musk behind it. But if my memory serves me right, he’s been very clear on his political stance from way back, and when the public thought his ties with President Donald Trump were going to benefit Tesla, the stock soared and doubled in value.

What I’m trying to say is that the hate train won’t last, and that was apparent with Tesla’s performance post deliveries missing estimates by a margin. However, the stock still shot up over 5% after Trump announced Musk’s departure from the DOGE soon.

I said this once, and I’ll say it again: I think this remains a good chance to keep adding in bunches before the incoming upside due to full self-driving, or FSD, in June and the new Model Y. I think negatives of the plunging Europe sales are now well priced into the stock, so if this month tells the same story, I believe it won’t make a dent on the stock price.

Stock price and valuation:

The stock is now down 29.5% year to date and is trading at $267, down over 44% from its mid-Jan highs of $479. I’m maintaining my buy on Tesla, and while I think there might be some more downside ahead due to the newly and freshly announced tariffs that sent the market on a nosedive Thursday, Tesla is still better positioned to be an outperformer in FY25 with expectations, so low at current levels. I’ll be discussing the tariff effect on Tesla in a second, but dare I say I appreciated Musk’s bluntness regarding tariffs late last month.

Wall Street is currently on the fence regarding the stock, with over 13% of Street analysts giving the stock a strong buy, 35.2% a buy, 29.6% a hold, and the rest a sell and a strong sell. Price target, or PT, mean and median have been on a downward trend over the past 3 months as well, with PT median down from $283 in January to $320 currently and PT mean also down from $284 to $318 currently. I would’ve personally flagged this if the number had stayed the same or maintained an upward trend. I think expectations are now reset.

The stock is down over 6% on the one-month chart, while forward P/E was up 2.6% and forward PEG ratio was up 46.3%. I think while the stock performance doesn’t reflect Tesla’s potential as an AI and robotics company, the PEG and P/E ratio do.

Tariffs:

A creator on X revealed in an infographic of “where cars sold in America are made” that Tesla was the only one that had “100% made in the U.S.,” as seen below. Musk responded to this saying: “Important to note that Tesla is NOT unscathed here. The tariff impact on Tesla is still significant.”

So yes, while Tesla might perhaps lose a finger, others are losing limbs. Tesla produces all its North American vehicles in the U.S. at factories in Texas and California, which could lessen the impact of tariffs in comparison to others dependent on manufacturing outside the U.S., like Ford (F), General Motors (GM), Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY), Toyota (TM), Honda (HMC), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF), Rivian (RIVN), and Stellantis (STLA). According to Cox Automotive’s director of industry insights, Tesla is better positioned against peers due to its vertical integration, where the company produces the vehicles, the batteries, and so on, putting them in control of the supply chain.

Basically, Tesla has a head start as of now since others still need to move production to the U.S., which not only costs a lot but is also a lengthy process. The fact is, EVs are already more expensive than gasoline-powered vehicles, making the odds of consumers picking them already slim. And now, the tariffs will definitely force companies to raise prices, causing demand, in my opinion, to be significantly hit. Tesla isn’t exempt, but the company has Model Y and Model 3 as the top 2 best-selling EV models in the U.S. last year, all while being significantly more expensive than others, snatching over 28.6% and 14.6% of the market share, respectively, as seen below.

Sure, plans for a cheaper model are now challenged, but I think the tariffs are now a priority, and maneuvering them will be an easier job for Tesla than the others. According to a document from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, around 20% to 25% of Tesla’s parts are imported from other countries, while the rest of the 60% to 75% are produced in the U.S. and Canada, as seen below.

Back in June, the Tesla Model 3 was on top of the list produced by Cars.com. According to the latest ranking from the Kogod School of Business, the 25 cars built in the U.S. that have the most American parts are the Tesla Model 3 at 87.5%, the Tesla Model Y Long Range, and the base Model Y tied for second place at 85%. Below is a list of the rest.

Tesla has been an apparent leader in the EV space, and a strong contender in the face of competition in the U.S. and China with players like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), Li Auto (LI), XPeng (XPEV), and Nio (NIO) who offer a pricing advantage. I don’t want to go off track, as I’ll discuss Tesla in China shortly. What I’m trying to say is that though the company is a recognized leader in EVs, it hasn’t had any new EV model aside from the Cybertruck since 2020. For a few earnings calls now, Musk has been talking about Tesla’s transformation to an AI robotics company, with robotaxis taking the spotlight. I think investors are starting to fully grasp that, and the deliveries miss, as I previously mentioned, yielded no substantial negative reaction from the market because eyes are now somewhere else. Call it the start of something new.

Tesla in China:

Tesla reported deliveries of 336,681, the lowest quarterly performance in over two years, down 13% year over year and missing estimates that have been revised down over the past few months from around 470,000 to 370,000.

Tesla dealt with partial shutdowns in some of its factories, and these pauses enabled the company to begin production of a revamped version of the Model Y, which has been very well received in both the U.S. and China (which the company started delivering in late February). I think this should help with better deliveries next quarter.

Tesla’s China-insured units have been soaring over the past few weeks, as seen below, which tells me demand there is healthy, despite strong competition.

What I’m watching:

  1. The Tesla-Canada situation. Back in late March, Canada froze rebate payments to Tesla, and two days ago, rumors and claims of Tesla being banned in Canada surfaced. Later, these were denied by Canadian government officials. Still, Tesla is now barred from future incentive programs, and I don’t think it will end there due to high tensions between Canada and the U.S. over tariffs.

  2. On the batteries side, which I discussed last time as Tesla’s new bread and butter, I’m optimistic about the upcoming Lithium Refinery Plant in Robstown, Texas, and think the company is best positioned due to the massive head start it has in that regard.

  3. I’m also watching Musk’s departure from his DOGE position, which caused the backlash against him. He’s expected to step back from his role once he finishes the 130-day period as a temporary advisor to Trump.

All eyes are now on the coming earnings call. JPMorgan reduced its estimates of the company’s earnings further and now expects Tesla’s first-quarter earnings to plunge to 36 cents per share, less than the previous projection of 40 cents per share, and analysts' estimates of 46 cents per share.

I upgrade Tesla to a Strong Buy and would advise investors to add on the pullback.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Melco Resorts & Entertainment: I'm Starting To See A Brighter Tomorrow
data.description:
Melco Resorts & Entertainment wants to penetrate Thailand

For Melco Resorts & Entertainment Limited (MLCO), my main investment thesis is that there will be a deal to sell City of Dreams Manila in the Philippines, and the company will use the free cash to build a casino in the emerging market of Thailand. Thailand should gradually, if the legalisation of gambling goes through, move into the top three in gambling operations. It has its quirks. However, I can see a potential Plan B play with MLCO with lower investment appreciation.

A little about the company

Melco Resorts & Entertainment is one of 6 casino operators in Macau that have been granted a valid license. The company also owns a majority stake in Studio City, Melco City of Dreams Mediterranean in Cyprus, and Melco City of Dreams Manila in the Philippines. MLCOs are not only casinos, they also have retail outlets, restaurants and bars, recreational facilities, spas, salons, and conference facilities. The company has been in business since 2004 and is based in Hong Kong.

Philippines vs. Thailand

MLCO faces a major challenge in the form of reallocation. Thailand is set to become the third-largest gambling area, behind Macau and Las Vegas.
