If we look at who currently occupies the third position, it is Singapore. However, Singapore has fought for this position with only two resorts. Thailand plans to allow five resorts in the first phase of the launch. The locations that will be in contention are also known and these are Chiang Mai, Phuket, Chonburi, and Bangkok. You can see that these are truly magnificent and attractive locations.

Thailand will most likely have a condition for a licensed capital of 300mil. USD, and a commitment to construction with a minimum value of 3 billion USD.

Why exactly change Manila for Thailand? If we look at MLCO's current portfolio of resorts, they are located in Macau, Sri Lanka, Cyprus, and the Philippines. We might as well drop the resorts in Macau, which are the golden vein, from our consideration.

Sri Lanka is awaiting opening in the third quarter of this year, and Cyprus has faced regional challenges, and upgrading. In addition, Manila has reported solid results for 2024, which of course may attract buyers and ensure a sufficient resort selling price. Thus, selling Manila seems like a logical step to get into Thailand.

We won't lie to our own pockets, MLCO can't currently find that spare cash anywhere thanks to the problems in Cyprus and high investment in Sri Lanka. So selling the Philippine resort seems to be the only way to penetrate the Thai market.

Manila's operating profit rose year-on-year to 138.8mil. USD from 120.5mil. USD. Other income saw a slight deterioration to 29.9mil. USD from 30.3mil. USD in 2023.

The company's debt irritates investors like a Red Bull, but…

According to the standard financial statements, the company is in bankruptcy, where the company's liabilities exceed its assets. Long-term debt of 7.135 billion USD looks ominous. Looking more closely at the various tranches, the company will be paying off 1 billion USD this year, and 0.5 billion USD next year. That sounds a bit more favourable considering that MLCO is currently sitting on about 1.147 billion USD in cash.

We have relatively enough time for rates to improve (there has been a reduction) for the U.S. dollar. The debt maturing in 2025 has the MLCO at 4.875%, and 2026 at 6%. If we look at the maturities of the next tranches in 2027 to 2029, we are looking at 5.25% to 7%. There could be room for refinancing at better terms.

Brighter tomorrows without Thailand, recovery

The company has suspended dividend payments to its investors in 2020. For many investors, this is a red flag, but for myself, I have to say that this was a manufacturing decision by management to manage a difficult financial situation. After almost 5 years, MLCO is finally getting into positive numbers after a partial Covid-19 recovery, and perhaps with an eye on a dividend resumption? Speaking for myself, I have to say that if a dividend were to be paid again, it would only be a nice bonus. And that's because the company has leaned relatively decently into share buybacks. In 2024, it repurchased nearly 62 million common shares in the market, worth $500 million. That's at a price of about 8 USD per share, not exactly the best price, but the more important fact is that they're still going. In the first two months of 2025, the company repurchased an additional 3.7 million shares (at a price of approximately 5.4 USD per share). The stock is at an attractive valuation, so there's nothing to do but firmly believe that the company will make good use of the remaining 368 million USD in repurchase rights. Based on this approach, the company is increasing shareholder value even without new market penetration. Manila is making money, and so is Macau. Cyprus should also start making money after the upgrade. And Sri Lanka which has been capital intensive should open 3Q this year. So the outlook is promising if we look at the recovery of the travel industry post COVID-19, the buyback program, refinancing, and decent management behavior.

Risks associated with the gambling industry

Investing in the gambling industry involves specific risks that can significantly affect our investment. Gambling is generally relatively sensitive to economic difficulties such as recession etc. Yes, we hear it every day that a recession is around the corner in America and may take other economies down with it. People are saving and entertaining heavily during this period, which has a high impact on the operation of gambling. In addition, investors during these periods have an increased aversion to risk and are more likely to experience capital outflows from these companies, further pushing the share price down.

Another perceived risk is regulation. The stability and predictability of the regulatory environment are number one for successful gambling operations. Changes in legislative laws, even tax laws, can have a significant impact on the operating profits of these companies. It is therefore necessary to closely monitor ongoing changes in the industry.

Even though we mentioned above that MLCO is one of the 6 licensees to operate such resorts, the competition is high in this industry. The market is saturated and there are plenty of substitutes. There is price rivalry, marketing costs have to increase, etc. All this affects the profit margin of the company.

A specific risk for MLCO is the failed sale of Manila, for which it wants approximately 1 billion USD. However, the potential buyer has said publicly that it is not thinking about it yet. It could be a business move where they want to put pressure on the price, or it could be genuine disinterest. If MLCO misses the opening of the market in Thailand, it will significantly start to fall behind its competitors and reduce the positive outlook for settling the high debt. Another question mark in the air is the buyback program and dividend. As long as the company is sensible in buying back shares at current valuations, there is nothing to argue, however we could see some failed buybacks in the form of 2024 which rather hurt us as investors.

Natural Element's

Thailand, among other things, contains a specific risk in the form of natural elements. I'm sure the vast majority of you have experienced the recent tragedy of the earthquake. Unfortunately, for Thailand, this is not unusual. The country is relatively frequently exposed to earthquakes of various levels, and among other risks, the risk comes from the ocean in the form of tsunamis. If we look at the metallurgical research on the Thai tectonic bed, we can experience in our case some sites being scrapped for new construction. If I look at the maps, certainly for MLCO the worst location would be Phuket in terms of natural elements. It is in the second danger zone for earthquakes, and also in the second danger zone for tsunamis. The location with the lowest risk for MLCO may then be in Chonburi, where the risk for the other locations is at a low level, but at the same time, the only location out of the four selected for the exhibition is in zone one for earthquakes. It should be considered that during the new construction or after its completion, additional capital expenditure may come. These may be covered in part by insurance in the event of a disaster, but will still occur spaciously in the form of reconstruction, and restoration of tourist peak times.

Conclusion

After evaluating the risks associated with this investment, I decided to issue a bullish sentiment on this stock. Despite being constantly bombarded with news regarding the coming recession and the associated cooling of demand for entertainment services, I believe the target price might not be far from the truth if the magic trick of selling Manila and entering the Thai market works out. If this primary move still doesn't work out, I'm fine with MLCO operating under the current Macau, Cyprus, and New Sri Lanka system. If they keep the buyback program at normal levels, there will be a recovery of the travel industry in these locations to pre-COVID-19 levels, so even the high debt may not be a problem.

data.title: Why Elme Communities Is A Buy At The Right Price
data.description:
It’s been a while since last covered Elme Communities (NYSE:ELME) back in April 2021. At that time, it was called Washington REIT and was focused on ownership of retail and office properties. Elme has undergone a serious transformation since that time, choosing to focus on multifamily properties, which have better supply and demand dynamics compared to offices.

That seems to have been a good call, given all of the tumult that office properties have experienced in the Washington, D.C. region amidst federal workforce layoffs this year. In this article, I revisit Elme in its current form and discuss whether the stock is a ‘Buy’ at present, so let’s get started!

Why ELME?

Elme Communities is a multifamily REIT that’s focused on the Washington, D.C. metro region and Atlanta. It has 9,400 apartment homes, 75% of which are in the D.C. metro region, and the remaining 25% are in Atlanta. Elme has just one commercial office building remaining in its portfolio, and that’s the Watergate 600 building. As shown below, this is an iconic building with a solid occupancy of 85% and a high-quality institutional tenant base.

Elme’s apartment communities tilt toward the affordability side, with 79% of them being categorized as Class B Value-Add. The remainder is 11% Class A/A- and 10% Class B. The affordable nature of Elme’s apartments is reflected by them having a 23% median rent-to-income ratio for new residents that moved in during 2024.

Having high exposure to the D.C. area my spook some investors amidst all the news of federal workforce layoffs. However, it’s worth noting that Elme’s direct exposure to non-DoD federal agency jobs is limited to just 6.2% of its residents. Another 4% of Elme’s residents have federal contractor jobs. This means that 90% of Elme’s residents have no direct exposure to the federal government. Notably, 97% of the job growth in the D.C. area over the past 12 months has been in industries other than the federal government.

Meanwhile, Elme is demonstrating solid portfolio fundamentals, with same-store occupancy rising by 20 basis points sequentially to 95% in Q4 and with retention rates above historical norms. Same-store blended lease growth was 1.3% YoY in Q4 and 1.8% YoY in January of this year, reflecting steady demand. As shown below, average occupancy has remained consistently high, while the bad debt expense ratio of 2% sits well below a recent peak of 3.5% from 2023.

Due to high interest rates, move-outs to homeownership remained low at 8.5%. This reflects the affordability appeal of Elme’s properties to its tenants. Moreover, Elme completed 500 full-unit renovations last year at an attractive ROI of 17%, and operational cost savings contributed to $1.8 million of incremental NOI last year. This was driven by the launch of a shared services department last year, which streamlined Elme’s resident account management, collections, and renewal processes into a single platform.

Management is guiding for steady multifamily revenue growth and expense growth of around 3%. Given that revenue and expenses are expected to largely offset one another, Core FFO per share is guided to be flat this year compared to 2024. Management also expects to achieve incremental NOI from the phased rollout of its managed Wi-Fi initiative for its residents, with costs rolled into the lease, as noted during the last earnings call:

Beyond our three-year target, we are in the process of rolling out managed Wi-Fi, and expect to capture $300,000 to $600,000 of additional NOI in 2025 from Phase 1 of our initiative, which includes seven communities.

Looking forward, once Phase 1 of our managed Wi-Fi initiative has been fully integrated into our lease role, which we expect to occur in mid-2026. We expect to capture approximately $1 million to $1.5 million of additional NOI per year, with further upside from future phases.

At the same time, Elme’s submarkets are seeing favorable dynamics with muted new supply compared to the U.S. and Sunbelt average, as shown below.

Elme carries a strong balance sheet with BBB/Baa2 credit ratings from S&P and Moody’s. 100% of Elme’s debt is unsecured, giving it balance sheet flexibility, and it has a safe net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 5.7x. As shown below, Elme’s debt maturities are staggered, with no maturing debt until 2026.

Elme currently yields a respectable 4.3% and the dividend is covered by an 81% payout ratio. At the current price of $16.66, Elme is reasonably priced with a forward P/FFO of 17.7. This sits about on par with that of apartment REIT peers AvalonBay Communities (AVB) 18.0 and Equity Residential’s (EQR) 17.2.

While I don’t believe now is a bad entry point for Elme, recent economic uncertainties in the D.C. metro area due to federal workforce layoffs may warrant some pause on the stock. Management noted low direct exposure to federal employees, but their layoffs could have collateral effects in the D.C. area among private payrolls. This, combined with what I would consider a ‘fair’ valuation for Elme results in my belief that Elme is a ‘Hold’ for now, as I believe shares are more attractive around the 15x P/FFO range due to near-term uncertainties.

Investor Takeaway

Elme Communities’ multifamily properties are seeing healthy occupancy and steady rent growth. It has limited direct exposure to federal jobs and strong operational execution. This includes cost-saving initiatives and high-return renovations, which support its long-term outlook. Given the broader economic uncertainty in the D.C. region and Elme’s current valuation near peer averages, the stock appears fairly priced. As such, Elme looks like a solid long-term name, but in the near term, it's best viewed as a Hold until a more attractive entry point emerges.
